focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 18

The bakery had gone too quiet for a room with dying lungs in it.

Cora woke on the narrow cot behind the counter with that knowledge already in her body. The flour sack she had thrown over herself in the middle of the night had slipped to her waist. Her mouth felt lined with grit. Under the worktable sat the valises they had packed after supper, the jar crate wrapped in a quilt, Dell's tool roll, Thea's books tied with clothesline. They had meant to leave after first light. The breadboards were scrubbed. The coffee pot had been filled the night before. Hester had said they would all think straighter after sleep, which was another way of saying nobody in that room had any sense left.

Now the bakery held the cold, dry smell it took on before dawn in late October, yeast gone flat, stove iron giving up what little heat it had kept, herbs in the rafters sharp as medicine. There was no cough from the back room, no muttered complaint, no spoon ringing the side of a cup.

Thea was standing in Hester's doorway in her stockings, one hand braced on the frame. Her hair had come loose down one side and made her face look younger and more frightened at once.

"Cora," she said, not loudly. "It's now."

Cora crossed the floor in two steps and felt every loose board under her bare feet because the room had gone so still. Dell was awake too, sitting up on the pallet he had made by the sacks, blanket around his shoulders, his bad left arm tucked close in the old habit he had never managed to break after the trench collapse. He did not speak. He looked as if speech would crack something and spill it.

Hester lay on top of the blanket instead of under it because she had spent the night too hot to bear weight and too cold to stop shivering. Somebody, probably Thea, had opened...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...d from outside for them to hurry if they meant to beat the road into town.

Cora pushed off the frame. There was no chair left to brace a hand on, no table to circle, no excuse remaining in the room. She crossed to Dell and held out her hand. He looked at it a second before taking it with his good one.

"Come on," she said. "We'll go to Hester's."

He got to his feet. Thea lifted the bag from the counter and stepped aside for them to pass, not servant, not stranger, simply making a place where there had not been one before. As Cora moved by, the back of her hand brushed Thea's sleeve. The contact was brief, almost nothing. It sent a live line through her all the same.

Outside, the yard had gone the color of dirty wool. The sold things were already becoming other people's burdens under tarps and rope. The house behind them, stripped and lawful and no longer hers, stood with its windows dulled back to glass, with no green water, no geese, no Ezra in a bank office. Truth had done its work and withdrawn, leaving them to the ordinary labor after revelation: loading what little remained, coughing in the wind, walking on.

Hester had the wagon turned crooked at the gate, cursing the left wheel under her breath with the concentration of a woman who reserved profanity for equipment and men who earned it. She wore Dell's bedroll slung over one shoulder and Cora's cedar chest already roped in place. Thea climbed up first to make room. Dell followed slowly, jaw tight with pain. Cora put one hand on the wagon board and looked back once at the porch.

The sale tag still hung from the empty nail where her Singer had stood. It beat itself against the clapboard in the rising wind.

Then she climbed up and let Hester drive them toward town while the first real edge of the storm came on.
